questions.question:
Heap allocation is required for languages
A. that support recursion
B. that support dynamic data structures
C. that use dynamic scope rules
D. None of the above
questions.answer: B. that support dynamic data structures
questions.to_find: Which language feature requires heap allocation?

understand.plain: A heap is a region of memory used for dynamic allocation at runtime. Languages that support dynamic data structures (like linked lists, trees, dynamically grown arrays) need to allocate and free memory at arbitrary points during execution, which requires a heap. Recursion uses the stack, not the heap.

keywords.explain: Allocation of memory from a pool (heap) at runtime, not at compile time. Memory can be allocated and freed in any order.
keywords.example: malloc() / new in C/C++ allocates from the heap

keywords.explain: A data structure whose size can grow or shrink at runtime (linked list, tree, graph).
keywords.example: A linked list node created with malloc() lives on the heap

keywords.explain: Activation records for function calls are placed on the stack; this supports recursion without needing the heap.
keywords.example: Each recursive call to factorial() pushes a new frame onto the stack

understand.visual_alt: Memory layout: stack grows downward for function calls (supports recursion), heap grows upward for dynamic allocation (required for dynamic data structures)

given.aim: Identify which language feature mandates heap (dynamic) memory allocation

terms.meaning: A memory region managed by the runtime for dynamic allocation/deallocation at arbitrary times
terms.example: malloc in C returns a pointer into the heap
terms.connects: Only needed when lifetime of data is not tied to a single function invocation

terms.meaning: A function calling itself; each call gets its own activation record on the run-time stack
terms.example: Factorial computed recursively uses only the stack
terms.connects: Stack (not heap) is sufficient for recursion

terms.meaning: Data structures that can grow/shrink at runtime with unpredictable lifetimes
terms.example: Linked list node created with new in Java
terms.connects: Requires heap because size/lifetime is unknown at compile time

given.plan: Eliminate options by checking which memory region (stack or heap) each feature actually requires. Recursion -> stack. Dynamic scope -> can use static or stack. Dynamic data structures -> must use heap.

steps.title: Why recursion does NOT require heap
steps.apply: Recursive languages use the run-time stack. Each function invocation creates an activation record pushed onto the stack and popped on return. No heap is needed solely for recursion.
steps.note: Languages like C, Pascal support recursion using only the stack

steps.title: Why dynamic scope rules do NOT require heap
steps.apply: Dynamic scoping determines variable bindings at runtime via the call chain. This can be implemented with the same stack of activation records; a heap is not strictly required.
steps.note: Dynamic scope affects variable lookup, not memory layout

steps.title: Why dynamic data structures REQUIRE heap
steps.apply: When a program creates objects (linked list nodes, trees, etc.) whose number and lifetime are not known at compile time, memory must be allocated from a heap pool and freed when no longer needed. Static or stack allocation cannot support this — stack memory is reclaimed on function return and static memory is fixed.
steps.note: This is the defining reason heap allocation exists in language runtimes

solution.result: Heap allocation is required for languages that support dynamic data structures. Answer: B.

questions.formula_note: Run-time memory model: stack for activation records (recursion), heap for dynamic allocation (dynamic data structures)

questions.question:
A language L allows declaration of arrays whose sizes are not known during compilation. It is required to make efficient use of memory. Which one of the following is true?
A. A compiler using static memory allocation can be written for L
B. A compiler cannot be written for L; an interpreter must be used
C. A compiler using dynamic memory allocation can be written for L
D. None of the above
questions.answer: C. A compiler using dynamic memory allocation can be written for L
questions.to_find: Which statement is true when array sizes are unknown at compile time?

understand.plain: If a language allows arrays whose sizes are determined at runtime (e.g., int a[n] where n is a runtime value), static memory allocation (fixed at compile time) cannot be used for those arrays. However, a compiler can still be written — it just needs to emit code that allocates memory dynamically (from the stack or heap) at runtime. An interpreter is not required.

keywords.explain: Memory size for all variables is determined and fixed at compile time.
keywords.example: int a[10]; — size 10 is fixed at compile time

keywords.meaning: Memory is allocated at runtime based on values computed during execution.
keywords.example: int a[n]; in C99 (VLA) — size n is known only at runtime

understand.visual_alt: Array with runtime-determined size n allocated on the stack or heap at the moment n becomes known

given.aim: Determine what kind of memory allocation strategy a compiler for language L must use

terms.meaning: A language that permits array declarations like 'array A[n]' where n is a runtime value
terms.connects: Sizes unknown at compile time rule out purely static allocation

terms.meaning: The compiler reserves fixed-size memory slots in the data segment or stack frame at compile time
terms.example: int x[10]; reserves 40 bytes at compile time
terms.connects: Cannot handle L because array size is not known at compile time

terms.meaning: Memory is allocated at runtime (from heap or stack via runtime routines)
terms.example: malloc(n * sizeof(int)) allocates n integers at runtime
terms.connects: Allows a compiler for L to emit runtime-allocation code

given.plan: Static allocation is ruled out since sizes are unknown. An interpreter is not required — a compiler can generate runtime allocation code. So a compiler with dynamic memory allocation can be written.

steps.title: Rule out static memory allocation
steps.apply: Static allocation requires all sizes to be fixed at compile time. Since array sizes in L are not known at compilation, the compiler cannot statically allocate them. Option A is false.
steps.note: Static allocation is used for global variables and fixed-size arrays

steps.title: Rule out interpreter requirement
steps.apply: There is no fundamental reason why only an interpreter can handle runtime-sized arrays. A compiler can emit machine code that calls runtime allocation routines (e.g., alloca for stack or malloc for heap). Option B is false.
steps.note: Many compiled languages (C99, Ada, Fortran) handle variable-length arrays via compiled code

steps.title: Confirm dynamic allocation works
steps.apply: A compiler for L can generate code that, at the point of array declaration, computes the size and calls a dynamic allocator. This makes efficient use of memory (only the needed amount is allocated) and satisfies the problem constraint. Option C is true.
steps.note: C99 variable-length arrays (VLAs) are a real-world example of this

solution.result: A compiler using dynamic memory allocation can be written for L. Answer: C.

questions.formula_note: Dynamic (runtime) memory allocation allows compiled code to handle arrays whose sizes are only known at runtime

questions.question:
In a resident OS computer, which of the following systems must reside in the main memory under all situations?
A. Assembler
B. Linker
C. Loader
D. Compiler
questions.to_find: Which system tool must always reside in main memory in a resident OS computer?

understand.plain: In a computer running a resident OS (where the OS itself lives in main memory), the loader is the component responsible for loading programs into memory for execution. Because every program that runs must go through the loader, the loader itself must always be in memory. The assembler, linker, and compiler are development tools used before execution and do not need to reside in memory during program execution.

keywords.explain: An operating system that permanently occupies a portion of main memory while the computer is running.
keywords.example: Early DOS kept the OS kernel resident; modern OSes keep the kernel in memory at all times

keywords.explain: A system program that reads an executable file from disk, loads it into memory, and starts its execution.
keywords.example: When you double-click a .exe file, the OS loader brings it into RAM

keywords.explain: A program that combines object modules into a single executable; it runs before execution, not during it.
keywords.example: ld in Linux links .o files into an executable

understand.visual_alt: Resident OS memory layout: OS kernel + Loader always in RAM; user program loaded by the loader; assembler/compiler/linker only needed during development

given.aim: Identify which system tool must permanently reside in main memory in a resident OS environment

terms.meaning: Translates assembly language into machine code; only needed during program development
terms.connects: Not needed during execution; can be swapped out

terms.meaning: Resolves external references and combines object files into an executable; done before execution
terms.connects: Run once before execution; not needed at runtime

terms.meaning: Loads the executable into memory and transfers control to it; needed every time a program is run
terms.connects: Must be in memory always since every program invocation requires it

given.plan: The loader is invoked every time any program is executed. Since programs must be loaded before running, the loader must always be present in memory. Other tools (assembler, linker, compiler) are used only during compilation/development and can reside on disk.

steps.title: Role of each tool
steps.apply: Assembler: source -> object code (development phase). Linker: object code -> executable (development phase). Compiler: source -> object code (development phase). Loader: executable -> running process (execution phase, every run).
steps.note: Only the loader is needed at runtime for every program

steps.title: Why loader must be resident
steps.apply: In a resident OS, the OS handles all program loading. To load any program (including a new copy of the assembler or compiler from disk), the loader must already be in memory. If the loader were on disk, it could not load itself. Hence it must always reside in main memory.
steps.note: This is analogous to why the OS kernel itself must be resident

solution.result: The Loader must always reside in main memory. Answer: C.

questions.formula_note: The loader is the bootstrap for every running program; it must be resident in memory at all times in a resident OS

questions.question:
A linker reads four modules whose lengths are 200, 300, 600 and 500 words, respectively. If they are loaded in that order, what are the relocation constants?
A. 0, 200, 500, 800
B. 0, 200, 500, 1100
C. 200, 700, 1300, 2100
D. 0, 200, 700, 1300
questions.to_find: Relocation constants for four modules of sizes 200, 300, 600, 500 loaded in order

understand.plain: When a linker loads multiple modules sequentially into memory, each module starts at the address immediately after the previous module ends. The relocation constant for each module is its starting address. Module 1 starts at 0, module 2 starts at 0+200=200, module 3 starts at 200+300=500, module 4 starts at 500+600=1100.

keywords.explain: The starting address assigned to a module when it is loaded into memory. All addresses within that module are adjusted by adding this constant.
keywords.example: If module 2 has relocation constant 200, an internal reference to address 5 becomes 200+5=205

keywords.explain: Combines multiple object modules into a single executable, resolving cross-module references and assigning load addresses.
keywords.example: A C program split into main.o and utils.o is linked into one executable

keywords.explain: Modules are packed into memory contiguously; each module's start address equals the sum of lengths of all preceding modules.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 520 120" width="520" height="120"><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x="10" y="30" width="80" height="60" class="bg"/><text x="50" y="55" text-anchor="middle" class="lbl ink">M1</text><text x="50" y="75" text-anchor="middle" class="sub mut">200</text><rect x="90" y="30" width="90" height="60" class="hi"/><text x="135" y="55" text-anchor="middle" class="lbl ink">M2</text><text x="135" y="75" text-anchor="middle" class="sub mut">300</text><rect x="180" y="30" width="120" height="60" class="bg"/><text x="240" y="55" text-anchor="middle" class="lbl ink">M3</text><text x="240" y="75" text-anchor="middle" class="sub mut">600</text><rect x="300" y="30" width="100" height="60" class="hi"/><text x="350" y="55" text-anchor="middle" class="lbl ink">M4</text><text x="350" y="75" text-anchor="middle" class="sub mut">500</text><text x="10" y="110" class="sub mut">0</text><text x="90" y="110" class="sub mut">200</text><text x="180" y="110" class="sub mut">500</text><text x="300" y="110" class="sub mut">1100</text><text x="400" y="110" class="sub mut">1600</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Memory layout: Module 1 (200 words) at 0, Module 2 (300 words) at 200, Module 3 (600 words) at 500, Module 4 (500 words) at 1100

given.aim: Compute relocation constants (starting addresses) for four modules loaded consecutively

terms.meaning: 200, 300, 600, 500 words for M1, M2, M3, M4 respectively
terms.connects: Each module's start = sum of all prior module lengths

terms.meaning: Starting load address = sum of lengths of modules M_1 through M_{i-1}
terms.connects: Used to relocate all internal addresses in the module

terms.meaning: Modules are placed back-to-back with no gaps
terms.example: M2 starts exactly where M1 ends
terms.connects: Guarantees relocation constants are simple cumulative sums

steps.title: Compute relocation constant for each module
steps.formula_raw: RC(M_i) = sum of sizes of M_1 ... M_{i-1}
steps.apply:
RC(M1) = 0 (first module always starts at 0)
RC(M2) = 0 + 200 = 200
RC(M3) = 200 + 300 = 500
RC(M4) = 500 + 600 = 1100
steps.note: The relocation constant is simply the cumulative offset where each module begins

steps.title: Match to options
steps.apply:
The relocation constants are 0, 200, 500, 1100. Checking the options:
A. 0,200,500,800 — wrong (M4 should start at 1100 not 800)
B. 0,200,500,1100 — correct
C. 200,700,1300,2100 — wrong (M1 should start at 0)
D. 0,200,700,1300 — wrong (M3 should start at 500 not 700)
steps.note: Option D would be correct if module sizes were 200, 500, 600, 500 — but M2 is 300 not 500

questions.formula_note: Relocation constant for module i = sum of sizes of all preceding modules (cumulative load offset)

questions.question:
Faster access to non-local variables is achieved using an array of pointers to activation records called a
A. stack
B. heap
C. display
D. activation tree
questions.to_find: What is the name of the array of pointers to activation records used for fast non-local variable access?

understand.plain: In block-structured languages with nested scopes, a function needs to access variables declared in enclosing scopes. One approach is to follow static (access) links through a chain of activation records — but this can be slow if the nesting is deep. A display is an array where display[i] holds a pointer to the most recent activation record at static nesting depth i, enabling O(1) access to non-local variables at any depth.

keywords.explain: An array of pointers, where display[i] points to the activation record of the most recently activated procedure at static nesting depth i. Gives O(1) access to non-local variables.
keywords.example: To access a variable at nesting depth 2, just dereference display[2]

keywords.explain: A block of memory (stack frame) created when a function is called, holding local variables, parameters, return address, etc.
keywords.example: When foo() is called, a new activation record for foo is pushed onto the stack

keywords.explain: A pointer in an activation record pointing to the activation record of the lexically enclosing scope. Following a chain of k static links to access a variable at depth k is O(k).
keywords.example: A function nested 3 levels deep must follow 3 static links to reach the outermost scope

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 400 160" width="400" height="160"><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><text x="10" y="20" class="lbl ink">Display array</text><rect x="10" y="30" width="80" height="35" class="bg"/><text x="50" y="52" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">display[0]</text><rect x="10" y="65" width="80" height="35" class="bg"/><text x="50" y="87" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">display[1]</text><rect x="10" y="100" width="80" height="35" class="hi"/><text x="50" y="122" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">display[2]</text><line x1="90" y1="47" x2="200" y2="47" stroke="#7F77DD" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#arr)"/><line x1="90" y1="82" x2="200" y2="82" stroke="#7F77DD" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#arr)"/><line x1="90" y1="117" x2="200" y2="117" stroke="#7F77DD" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#arr)"/><rect x="200" y="30" width="120" height="35" class="bg"/><text x="260" y="52" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">AR depth 0</text><rect x="200" y="65" width="120" height="35" class="bg"/><text x="260" y="87" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">AR depth 1</text><rect x="200" y="100" width="120" height="35" class="hi"/><text x="260" y="122" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">AR depth 2</text><defs><marker id="arr" markerWidth="8" markerHeight="8" refX="6" refY="3" orient="auto"><path d="M0,0 L0,6 L8,3 z" fill="#7F77DD"/></marker></defs></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Display array: display[0] -> activation record at nesting depth 0; display[1] -> AR at depth 1; display[2] -> AR at depth 2. Direct pointer access in O(1).

given.aim: Name the runtime data structure that provides fast O(1) access to non-local variables via an array of activation record pointers

terms.meaning: A variable declared in an enclosing lexical scope, not the current function's own scope
terms.example: In Pascal, a variable declared in the outer procedure accessed by an inner procedure
terms.connects: To access it, one must find the right activation record in the call chain

terms.meaning: Global array indexed by nesting depth; display[d] points to the most recent AR at depth d
terms.example: display[2] always points to the latest activation of the depth-2 procedure
terms.connects: Directly indexes to the correct AR in O(1), unlike static-link chains

terms.meaning: Runtime data block for a function invocation: parameters, locals, return address, static/dynamic links
terms.example: Every call to a procedure allocates an AR on the stack
terms.connects: Display stores pointers to these ARs for each nesting level

given.plan: Recall that in runtime environment implementations, the 'display' is the standard term for the array of pointers to activation records used for O(1) non-local variable access. Static links require following a chain; display avoids this.

steps.title: Analyze each option
steps.apply:
A. Stack: the call stack holds activation records but does not provide fast non-local access.
B. Heap: used for dynamic allocation, unrelated to activation record indexing.
C. Display: an array display[0..max_depth] where display[d] = pointer to the current AR at nesting depth d. Gives O(1) access to any non-local variable.
D. Activation tree: conceptual tree showing all possible invocations; not a runtime array of pointers.
steps.note: The term 'display' was introduced by Dijkstra for ALGOL to speed up non-local variable access

steps.title: Confirm display semantics
steps.formula_raw: non-local variable at depth d -> *(display[d] + offset)
steps.apply: To access a variable at static nesting depth d with offset k in its AR: fetch display[d] (one array access), then add offset k. This is O(1) regardless of how deep the nesting is. Without a display, one would follow static_link pointers level by level, costing O(d).
steps.note: Maintained by updating display[current_depth] on every procedure entry/exit

solution.result: The array of pointers to activation records used for faster non-local variable access is called a display. Answer: C.

questions.formula_note: Display: array indexed by static nesting depth, each entry points to the most recent activation record at that depth; enables O(1) non-local variable access

questions.question:
The process of assigning load addresses to the various parts of the program and adjusting the code and the data in the program to reflect the assigned addresses is called
A. Assembly
B. Parsing
C. Relocation
D. Symbol resolution
questions.to_find: What is the term for assigning load addresses and adjusting code/data to reflect those addresses?

understand.plain: When a program is compiled, addresses in the code may be written relative to a base address of 0 or some other reference. When the linker or loader places the program at a specific address in memory, it must update all address references in the code and data to reflect the actual load address. This process of assigning load addresses and patching all address-dependent fields is called relocation.

keywords.explain: The process of adjusting address references in a program to correspond to the actual memory addresses where the program is loaded.
keywords.example: If a module compiled to start at 0 is loaded at address 500, all internal addresses must be incremented by 500

keywords.explain: The linker step where external symbol references (calls to functions in other modules) are resolved to actual addresses.
keywords.example: A call to printf in main.o is resolved to the actual address of printf in the C library

keywords.explain: The actual memory address at which a program or module begins execution.
keywords.example: A module with relocation constant 200 has load address 200

understand.visual_alt: Relocation: compiled code uses addresses relative to 0; at load time, a relocation constant is added to all address fields to produce actual runtime addresses

given.aim: Identify the correct term for assigning load addresses and adjusting code/data accordingly

terms.meaning: Translation of assembly language mnemonics into machine code by an assembler
terms.example: MOV AX, BX -> binary opcode bytes
terms.connects: Unrelated to the address-adjustment process described

terms.meaning: Assigning actual load addresses to program segments and patching all address-dependent fields to reflect those addresses
terms.example: Adding relocation constant 500 to all address fields in a module loaded at 500
terms.connects: Exactly matches the definition in the question

terms.meaning: Matching undefined symbol references in one module to symbol definitions in another
terms.example: Linking printf reference in main.o with definition in libc.a
terms.connects: Related to linking but specifically about resolving symbolic names, not adjusting addresses

given.plan: Match the definition in the question ('assigning load addresses and adjusting code/data to reflect assigned addresses') to the standard linker/loader terminology. This is the textbook definition of relocation.

steps.title: Eliminate incorrect options
steps.apply:
A. Assembly: translating assembly language to machine code — not address assignment.
B. Parsing: analyzing source code syntax — completely unrelated.
D. Symbol resolution: resolving external symbolic references to addresses — partial process of linking, but not the address-adjustment process itself.
steps.note: Symbol resolution determines WHERE something is; relocation PATCHES all references to point there

steps.title: Confirm relocation
steps.apply: Relocation is the two-step process: (1) assign a load address (relocation constant) to each program segment; (2) scan through the code and data, find all relocatable address fields, and add the relocation constant to each. This is exactly what the question describes.
steps.note: Relocation information (a list of relocatable fields) is stored in the object file's relocation table

solution.result: The process described is called Relocation. Answer: C.

questions.formula_note: Relocation: patched_address = original_address + relocation_constant; performed by linker or loader when placing program at a specific memory address
